title: Finding quartiles and IQR
problem: The number of hours of screen time recorded for 7 students, listed in order, are: $5, 9, 13, 17, 21, 25, 29$. Find $Q_1$, $Q_2$, $Q_3$ and the IQR.

steps.instruction: Count the values and locate the median ($Q_2$).
steps.working:
There are 7 values. The median is the middle (4th) value.

$$5, 9, 13, \mathbf{17}, 21, 25, 29$$

$Q_2 = 17$

steps.instruction: Identify the lower half (values below $Q_2$) and find $Q_1$.
steps.working:
Lower half: $5, 9, 13$

$Q_1$ is the middle value of the lower half: $Q_1 = 9$

steps.instruction: Identify the upper half (values above $Q_2$) and find $Q_3$.
steps.working:
Upper half: $21, 25, 29$

$Q_3$ is the middle value of the upper half: $Q_3 = 25$

title: Five-number summary and outlier test
problem: A dataset of 8 values, listed in order, is: $10, 14, 18, 22, 26, 30, 34, 60$. Construct the five-number summary and determine whether 60 is an outlier.

steps.instruction: Find the median ($Q_2$). With 8 values, it is the average of the 4th and 5th values.
Find $Q_1$ as the median of the lower half: $10, 14, 18, 22$.
Find $Q_3$ as the median of the upper half: $26, 30, 34, 60$.
Calculate the IQR and find the upper fence.
Compare the maximum value to the upper fence.
steps.working: $60 > 56$, so the value 60 lies beyond the upper fence. It is an outlier.

answer: Five-number summary: $\{10, 16, 24, 32, 60\}$. The value 60 is an outlier (upper fence $= 56$).

title: Interpreting parallel box plots and the effect of removing an outlier
problem:
Two datasets are displayed as parallel box plots with the following five-number summaries.

Dataset A: min $= 20$, $Q_1 = 35$, $Q_2 = 45$, $Q_3 = 65$, max $= 80$

Dataset B: min $= 30$, $Q_1 = 50$, $Q_2 = 65$, $Q_3 = 75$, max $= 85$

(a) Compare the centre, spread and shape of the two distributions.

(b) Dataset B consists of 6 values with a mean of 62. The value 85 is removed as an outlier. Find the mean of the remaining 5 values.

steps.working:
$Q_2$ for Dataset A $= 45$; $Q_2$ for Dataset B $= 65$.

Dataset B has a higher centre — a typical value in Dataset B is about 20 units higher than in Dataset A.

steps.instruction: Compare the IQRs (spread of the middle 50%).
steps.working:
$$\text{IQR}_A = 65 - 35 = 30$$

$$\text{IQR}_B = 75 - 50 = 25$$

Dataset A has a greater IQR, so its middle 50% of values are more spread out.

steps.instruction: Assess the shape of Dataset A.
steps.working:
Distance from $Q_1$ to $Q_2$: $45 - 35 = 10$

Distance from $Q_2$ to $Q_3$: $65 - 45 = 20$

The median is closer to $Q_1$, indicating a longer upper section. Dataset A is positively skewed.

steps.instruction: Assess the shape of Dataset B.
steps.working:
Distance from $Q_1$ to $Q_2$: $65 - 50 = 15$

Distance from $Q_2$ to $Q_3$: $75 - 65 = 10$

The median is closer to $Q_3$, indicating a longer lower section. Dataset B is negatively skewed.

steps.instruction: Find the total sum for Dataset B before removing the outlier.
Remove the outlier and find the new mean.
steps.working:
$$\text{New sum} = 372 - 85 = 287$$

$$\text{New mean} = \frac{287}{5} = 57.4$$

Removing the outlier of 85 reduces the mean from 62 to 57.4.

answer: Dataset B has a higher median; Dataset A has a greater IQR. Dataset A is positively skewed; Dataset B is negatively skewed. After removing 85, the mean of Dataset B is $57.4$.
